question_text: Which of the following pairs of ions are isoelectronic and isostructural? {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}, \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}, \mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-}, \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
question_html:
Which of the following pairs of ions are isoelectronic and isostructural?

  1. {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}, \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}, \mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-}, \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}

options.explanation: {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} , Number of electrons = 6 + 3(8) + 2 = 32 According to VSEPR theory it is trigonal planar in shape. NO 3- , Number of electrons = 7 + 3(8) + 1 = 32 According to VSEPR theory it is trigonal planar in shape. ClO 3 - , Number of electrons = 17 + 3(8) + 1 = 42 According to VSEPR theory, it is trigonal pyramidal in shape. SO 3 2- , Number of electrons = 16 + 3(8) + 2 = 42 According to VSEPR theory, it is trigonal pyramidal in shape. Thus, CO 3 2- , NO 3 - and CIO 3 - , SO 3 2- are isoelectronic

question_text: Which of the following statements are correct? Among halogens, radius ratio between iodine and fluorine is maximum. Leaving F—F bond, all halogens have weaker X—X bond than X—X' bond in interhalogens. Among interhalogen compounds maximum number of atoms are present in iodine fluoride. Interhalogen compounds are more reactive than halogen compounds.
question_html:
Which of the following statements are correct?

  1. Among halogens, radius ratio between iodine and fluorine is maximum.
  2. Leaving F—F bond, all halogens have weaker X—X bond than X—X' bond in interhalogens.
  3. Among interhalogen compounds maximum number of atoms are present in iodine fluoride.
  4. Interhalogen compounds are more reactive than halogen compounds.
solution_text: (i), (iii) and (iv) Explanation: Among halogen radius ratio between iodine and fluorine is maximum because iodine has a maximum radius while fluorine has a minimum radius as atomic radii increases from fluorine to Iodine. Also, due to the highest radius ratio, the maximum number of atoms is present in iodine fluoride. Interhalogen compounds are more reactive than halogen compounds because A - B bond of dissimilar halogen is weaker than A - A or B - B bond of halogens.
solution_html:
(i), (iii) and (iv)
Explanation: Among halogen radius ratio between iodine and fluorine is maximum because iodine has a maximum radius while fluorine has a minimum radius as atomic radii increases from fluorine to Iodine. Also, due to the highest radius ratio, the maximum number of atoms is present in iodine fluoride. Interhalogen compounds are more reactive than halogen compounds because A - B bond of dissimilar halogen is weaker than A - A or B - B bond of halogens.

solution_text: In PCl 5 phosphorus undergoes sp 3 d hybridization and a trigonal bipyramidal configuration comes into existence. The three equatorial P-Cl bonds are equivalent while two axial bonds are different and longer than equatorial bonds. Therefore, all 5 bonds are not equivalent.

question_html: Out of H2O and H2S, which one has higher bond angle and why?
solution_text: Oxygen is more electronegative than sulphur therefore, bond pair of electrons of O-H bond will be closer to oxygen. As a result, there will be more bond pair-bond pair repulsion between bond pairs of two O-H bonds resulting in a higher bond angle(104). So the bond angle of H 2 O more than H 2 S.

question_text: SF 6 is known but SCl 6 is not. Why?
question_html: SF6 is known but SCl6 is not. Why?
solution_text: Due to small size, six fluorine atoms can be accommodated around sulphur atom while chlorine atoms being larger in size are difficult to accommodate around sulphur. The other reason is that the fluorine being highly electronegative and oxidising in nature is capable of unpairing the paired orbitals of the values shell of sulphur atom and thereby showing the highest, oxidation state of +6 while chlorine is not able to do this. therefore, SF 6 is known but SCl 6 is not known.

question_text: Explain why the stability of oxoacids of chlorine increases in the order given below: HClO < HClO 2 < HClO 3 < HClO 4
question_html:
Explain why the stability of oxoacids of chlorine increases in the order given below:
HClO < HClO2 < HClO3 < HClO4
solution_text: The more oxygen atom that is bonded with the oxoacids the electrons will be pulled away from the O-H bond, and the more this bond will be weakened. Thus HClO 4 requires the least energy to break the O-H bond, release H + ion which lead to the formation of ClO - 4. Hence HClO 4 is the strongest acid, and the order of stability is HClO < HClO 2 < HClO 3 < HClO 4 .
solution_html: The more oxygen atom that is bonded with the oxoacids the electrons will be pulled away from the O-H bond, and the more this bond will be weakened. Thus HClO4 requires the least energy to break the O-H bond, release H+ ion which lead to the formation of ClO-4. Hence HClO4 is the strongest acid, and the order of stability is HClO < HClO2 < HClO3 < HClO4.

question_text: Explain why ozone is thermodynamically less stable than oxygen.
question_html: Explain why ozone is thermodynamically less stable than oxygen.
solution_text: Ozone is thermodynamically unstable with respect to oxygen since its decomposition into oxygen results in the liberation of heat ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} H is negative) and an increase in entropy ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} S is positive). These two effects reinforce each other, resulting in large negative Gibbs energy change ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} G) for its conversion into oxygen.

These two effects reinforce each other, resulting in large negative Gibbs energy change ({tex}\Delta{/tex}G) for its conversion into oxygen.

question_html: P4O6 reacts with water according to equation P4O6 + 6H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4H3PO3. Calculate the volume of 0.1 M NaOH solution required to neutralize the acid formed by dissolving 1.1 g of P4O6 in H2O.
solution_text: P 4 O 6 + 6H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4H 3 PO 3 .....(i) For neutralisation 4 {tex}\times{/tex} H 3 PO 3 + 2NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na 2 HPO 3 + 2H 2 O ....(ii) Adding equation (i) and (ii) P 4 O 6 + 8NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4Na 2 HPO 3 + 2H 2 O P 4 O 6 (mol. mass) = (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 31 + 16 {tex}\times{/tex} 6) = 220 Number of moles of P 4 O 6 = {tex}\frac{ Givenmass } { Molar mass }{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.1}{220}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} The product formed by {tex}\frac{1.1}{220}{/tex} of P 4 O 6 will be neutralised by 8 moiles of NaOH {tex}\therefore{/tex} The product formed by {tex}\frac{1.1}{220}{/tex} of P 4 O 6 will be neutralised by NaOH. P 4 O 6 = {tex}8 x=\frac{1.1}{220}=\frac{8.8}{220}{/tex} mole NaOH (Given molarity of NaOH in 1L = 0.1 M) Molarity = {tex}\frac{\text { No. of moles }}{\text { Volume in litres }}{/tex} Volume of 0.1 M of NaOH Volume = {tex}\frac{\text { No. of moles }}{\text { Molarity }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8.8}{220} \times \frac{1}{0.1}{/tex} = 0.4 L
solution_html:
P4O6 + 6H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4H3PO3 .....(i)
For neutralisation
4 {tex}\times{/tex} H3PO3 + 2NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Na2HPO3 + 2H2O ....(ii)
Adding equation (i) and (ii)
P4O6 + 8NaOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4Na2HPO3 + 2H2O
P4O6(mol. mass) = (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 31 + 16 {tex}\times{/tex} 6) = 220
Number of moles of 
P4O6 = {tex}\frac{ Givenmass } { Molar mass }{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1.1}{220}{/tex} 
{tex}\because{/tex} The product formed by {tex}\frac{1.1}{220}{/tex} of P4O6 will be neutralised by 8 moiles of NaOH
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The product formed by {tex}\frac{1.1}{220}{/tex} of P4O6 will be neutralised by NaOH.
P4O6 = {tex}8 x=\frac{1.1}{220}=\frac{8.8}{220}{/tex} mole NaOH (Given molarity of NaOH in 1L = 0.1 M)
Molarity = {tex}\frac{\text { No. of moles }}{\text { Volume in litres }}{/tex} 

Volume of 0.1 M of NaOH
Volume = {tex}\frac{\text { No. of moles }}{\text { Molarity }}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{8.8}{220} \times \frac{1}{0.1}{/tex} = 0.4 L

  1. white phosphorus
  2. red phosphorus and
  3. black phosphorus.

Write the difference between white and red phosphorus on the basis of their structure and reactivity.
solution_text: Structure: White phosphorus is a discrete tetrahedral molecule. Thus it has a tetrahedral structure with 6 P–P bonds. Structure of white phosphorus. In red P, several P 4 tetrahedral molecules are linked to the formed polymeric chain. Structure of red phosphorus. Reactivity: White phosphorus is much more reactive than red phosphorus. This is because in white phosphorus there is an angular strain in P 4 molecules because the bond angles is only 60°.
solution_html:

      Structure of white phosphorus.
    2. In red P, several P4 tetrahedral molecules are linked to the formed polymeric chain. 

      Structure of red phosphorus.
  2. Reactivity:
    White phosphorus is much more reactive than red phosphorus. This is because in white phosphorus there is an angular strain in P4 molecules because the bond angles is only 60°.

question_text: An amorphous solid A burns in air to form a gas B which turns lime water milky. The gas is also produced as a by-product during roasting of sulphide ore. This gas decolourises acidified aqueous KMnO 4 solution and reduces Fe 3+ to Fe 2+ . Identify the solid A and the gas B and write the reactions involved.
question_html: An amorphous solid A burns in air to form a gas B which turns lime water milky. The gas is also produced as a by-product during roasting of sulphide ore. This gas decolourises acidified aqueous KMnO4 solution and reduces Fe3+ to Fe2+. Identify the solid A and the gas B and write the reactions involved.
solution_text: A is sulphur (S) solid while B is sulphur dioxide (SO 2 ) gas.
solution_html: A is sulphur (S) solid while B is sulphur dioxide (SO2) gas.

question_text: On heating lead (II) nitrate gives a brown gas A . The gas A on cooling changes to colourless solid B . Solid B on heating with NO changes to a blue solid C . Identify A, B and C and also write reactions involved and draw the structures of B and C.
question_html: On heating lead (II) nitrate gives a brown gas A. The gas A on cooling changes to colourless solid B. Solid B on heating with NO changes to a blue solid C. Identify A, B and C and also write reactions involved and draw the structures of B and C.
solution_text: The gas A is Nitrogen oxide NO 2 . The reactions are explained as under: {tex}2Pb{(N{O_3})_2}\xrightarrow[{673K}]{{Heat}}2PbO + \mathop {4N{O_2}}\limits_{\mathop {\mathop {Nitrogen\,dioxide}\limits^{(A)} }\limits_{(Brown\,\,colour)} } + {O_2}{/tex} The brown gas A on cooling dimerizes to give B therefore, B is N 2 O 4 . {tex}2N{O_2}\xrightarrow{{Cooling}}\mathop {{N_2}{O_4}}\limits_{_{\mathop {(B)}\limits_{Solid\,\,(Colourless)} }} {/tex} Solid B on heating with NO gives blue solid C therefore, C is N 2 O 3 . {tex}2NO + {N_2}{O_4}\xrightarrow{{Heating}}\mathop {2{N_2}{O_3}}\limits_{\mathop {blue\,\,solid}\limits_{\mathop {(C)}\limits_{Dinitrogen\,\,trioxide} } } {/tex} Structure B Structure of C
solution_html:
The gas A is Nitrogen oxide NO2. The reactions are explained as under:
{tex}2Pb{(N{O_3})_2}\xrightarrow[{673K}]{{Heat}}2PbO + \mathop {4N{O_2}}\limits_{\mathop {\mathop {Nitrogen\,dioxide}\limits^{(A)} }\limits_{(Brown\,\,colour)} } + {O_2}{/tex}  
The brown gas A on cooling dimerizes to give B therefore, B is N2O4.
{tex}2N{O_2}\xrightarrow{{Cooling}}\mathop {{N_2}{O_4}}\limits_{_{\mathop {(B)}\limits_{Solid\,\,(Colourless)} }} {/tex}
Solid B on heating with NO gives blue solid C therefore, C is N2O3.
{tex}2NO + {N_2}{O_4}\xrightarrow{{Heating}}\mathop {2{N_2}{O_3}}\limits_{\mathop {blue\,\,solid}\limits_{\mathop {(C)}\limits_{Dinitrogen\,\,trioxide} } } {/tex} 
Structure B

Structure of C

subject: Chemistry
book: Class 12 - Chemistry - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: On heating compound (A) gives a gas (B) which is a constituent of air. This gas when treated with 3 mol of hydrogen (H 2 ) in the presence of a catalyst gives another gas (C) which is basic in nature. Gas C on further oxidation in moist condition gives a compound (D) which is a part of acid rain. Identify compounds (A) to (D) and also give necessary equations of all the steps involved.
question_html: On heating compound (A) gives a gas (B) which is a constituent of air. This gas when treated with 3 mol of hydrogen (H2) in the presence of a catalyst gives another gas (C) which is basic in nature. Gas C on further oxidation in moist condition gives a compound (D) which is a part of acid rain. Identify compounds (A) to (D) and also give necessary equations of all the steps involved.
solution_text: Compound (A) = NH 4 NO 2 , (B) = N 2 , (C) = NH 3 , (D) = HNO 3 {tex}\mathop {N{H_4}N{O_2}}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{Heat}}\mathop {{N_2}}\limits_{(B)} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}{N_2} + 3{H_2}\xrightarrow{{Catalyst}}\mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_{(C)} {/tex} 4NH 3 + 5O 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO + 6H 2 O 2NO + O 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO 2 3NO 2 + H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{(D)}2HNO_3{/tex} + NO
solution_html:
Compound (A) = NH4NO2, (B) = N2, (C) = NH3, (D) = HNO3 
{tex}\mathop {N{H_4}N{O_2}}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{Heat}}\mathop {{N_2}}\limits_{(B)} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} 
{tex}{N_2} + 3{H_2}\xrightarrow{{Catalyst}}\mathop {2N{H_3}}\limits_{(C)} {/tex} 
4NH3 + 5O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO + 6H2O
2NO + O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2NO2 
3NO2 + H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{(D)}2HNO_3{/tex} + NO
